title: PCA to close Richmond packaging plant
content:
Packaging Corporation of America (PCA) is shutting its converting plant in Richmond, Virginia, resulting in the loss of 110 jobs, effective June.

In a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) filing, the Illinois-based containerboard producer said it will coordinate with state and local authorities in Virginia on support for employees who lose their jobs.

The company also said it will assist workers interested in relocating to other PCA sites.

Mark Romaniuk, deputy general counsel at the company, described the move as “a difficult business decision.”

The notice said that hourly staff in production and maintenance roles at the Richmond facility are represented by the United Steel, Paper and Forestry, Rubber, Manufacturing, Energy, Allied Industrial and Service Workers International Union.

PCA also referenced a satellite warehouse in North Chesterfield, Virginia, which employs six people.

“PCA intends to continue operating the warehouse and will engage in discussions with USW regarding ongoing staffing of the facility,” the letter stated.

The decision to close the Richmond plant follows downsizing in Washington state.

Last year, PCA announced it would permanently take out of service the No. 2 paper machine and kraft pulping operations at its containerboard mill in Wallula, Washington, a step that led to 200 job cuts.

Alongside these reductions, the company has been investing in other parts of its network, including facilities it acquired from Greif last year.

In late 2025, PCA closed corrugated packaging plants in Allentown, Pennsylvania, and Salisbury, North Carolina, on 1 December and 19 December, respectively.

The closures affected 168 jobs - 60 in Allentown and 108 in Salisbury.

"PCA to close Richmond packaging plant" was originally created and published by Packaging Gateway, a GlobalData owned brand.

title: Is It Too Late To Consider Packaging Corporation of America (PKG) After The Recent Pullback
content:
Find your next quality investment with Simply Wall St's easy and powerful screener, trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Wondering if Packaging Corporation of America at around US$204 a share still offers value, or if most of the opportunity is already reflected in the price? This article breaks down what the current valuation really says about the stock. The share price has pulled back recently, with a 3.7% decline over the last week and a 9.6% decline over the last month, even though the 1 year return sits at 14.2% and the 5 year return at 72.0%. Recent coverage has focused on how the stock's longer term returns of 57.1% over 3 years and 72.0% over 5 years compare to its more recent 3.2% year to date decline. This has raised questions about whether the latest moves are a pause or a signal of shifting expectations. This mix of performance data is prompting closer attention to what investors are currently paying for the business. Simply Wall St's valuation model currently gives Packaging Corporation of America a valuation score of 1 out of 6. The next sections will walk through the key valuation approaches behind that outcome and then finish with a broader way to think about what the stock is really worth.

Packaging Corporation of America scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Packaging Corporation of America Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a single present value figure.

For Packaging Corporation of America, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $854.7 million. Analyst estimates feed into the earlier years, then Simply Wall St extrapolates further out, with projected free cash flow of around $1,109.7 million in 2035, all expressed in $ and discounted back to today.

Adding these discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $252.99 per share. Compared with a current share price around $204, the model implies the stock trades at roughly a 19.2% discount to this DCF value. On this measure, the shares appear to be trading below the model's estimate of intrinsic value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Packaging Corporation of America is undervalued by 19.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 59 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Story Continues

PKG Discounted Cash Flow as at Apr 2026

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Packaging Corporation of America.

Approach 2: Packaging Corporation of America Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for a share to the earnings that support it, which is why it is a common anchor for valuation work.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risks. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually call for a lower one.

Packaging Corporation of America currently trades on a P/E of 23.51x. That is above the Packaging industry average P/E of 15.39x and very close to the peer average of 23.39x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for the company is 22.85x, which is its view of what the P/E "should" be given factors such as earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio offers a more tailored yardstick than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific characteristics rather than assuming every packager should trade on the same multiple. Against this Fair Ratio, the current 23.51x P/E is moderately higher. This points to the shares being priced above that model based benchmark.

Result: OVERVALUEDNYSE:PKG P/E Ratio as at Apr 2026

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 20 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Packaging Corporation of America Narrative

Earlier there was mention of a better way to think about valuation, and that is where Narratives come in, giving you a simple way to attach your own story for Packaging Corporation of America to the numbers. This works by linking a view on its future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast, a fair value, and a clear comparison with the current share price, all within Simply Wall St's Community page. Narratives are refreshed when new news or earnings arrive. For Packaging Corporation of America, you might see one Narrative built around a Fair Value of about US$270.00 that assumes revenue near US$11.5b and earnings of about US$1.3b by 2029 with a P/E of 21.7x. Alongside that, there may be another Narrative closer to US$160.12 that works off revenue of roughly US$9.4b and earnings of about US$1.1b by 2028 with a P/E of 15.9x. Your job is to decide which story feels closer to your own view and whether that makes the current price look high, low, or roughly in line.

For Packaging Corporation of America, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Packaging Corporation of America Narratives:

🐂 Packaging Corporation of America Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish Narrative: US$270.00

Implied discount to that fair value at the recent US$204.46 share price: about 24.3% undervalued

Revenue growth assumption used: 8.54% a year

Assumes revenue of about US$11.5b and earnings of roughly US$1.3b by 2029, with profit margins rising compared to today. Works off a future P/E of 21.7x, paired with disciplined supply management, synergy benefits from the Greif deal and improving free cash flow. Sees Packaging Corporation of America benefiting from greater recycled fiber exposure and sustainability focused packaging demand, while acknowledging risks from regulation, capital intensity and customer concentration.

🐻 Packaging Corporation of America Bear Case

Fair value in this bearish Narrative: about US$167.95

Implied premium to that fair value at the recent US$204.46 share price: about 21.8% overvalued

Revenue growth assumption used: 6.10% a year

Builds around earnings of about US$1.1b by 2028 on revenue of roughly US$9.4b, with a future P/E of 15.9x that sits below current industry levels. Flags long term pressure from digitalization, alternative packaging materials, aging capital intensive mills and heavier regulation on single use packaging. Accepts that e commerce, supply discipline and the Greif acquisition can support results, but still treats the current market valuation as too optimistic for these assumptions.

title: The Bull Case For Packaging Corporation of America (PKG) Could Change Following Richmond Plant Closure Announcement
content:
Packaging Corporation of America has announced the closure of its Richmond, Virginia converting plant effective June 2026, affecting 110 employees, while maintaining operations at a nearby satellite warehouse in North Chesterfield. This operational shift, coinciding with fresh analyst coverage, raises questions about how plant consolidation and capacity decisions may influence the company’s longer-term positioning. We’ll now explore how the Richmond plant closure, and the capacity and cost decisions behind it, may reshape Packaging Corporation of America’s investment narrative.

Find 59 companies with promising cash flow potential yet trading below their fair value.

Packaging Corporation of America Investment Narrative Recap

To own Packaging Corporation of America, you have to believe in steady demand for containerboard and paper, supported by disciplined capacity and cost management. The Richmond, Virginia plant closure looks like a targeted operational move rather than a shift in that broader thesis, though it highlights execution risk around matching supply to demand and keeping margins healthy amid cost pressures and economic uncertainty.

The most relevant recent development alongside the Richmond news is Deutsche Bank’s initiation of coverage with a “hold” rating and a US$225 target price. Coming shortly after a 3.9% share price drop, this underlines how expectations for earnings growth and margin resilience are already finely balanced, and how any capacity actions could influence how near term catalysts and risks are viewed as new information emerges.

Yet, while bullish analysts were previously modeling US$11.5 billion of revenue and US$1.3 billion of earnings by 2029, investors should also be aware that...
